entry.title: Re: Deleting records from replicated table
entry.content: Hi, thanks for the reply, not sure whether I'm misunderstanding your suggestion, but this isn't a replication flow. It's real-time replication via a remote table, set up within a graphical view. Datasphere has created insert, update and delete triggers on the remote tables but clearly doesn't capture data removal as a result of truncate commands.

entry.title: SAP RPT-1 - Why Enterprise AI Needs More Than Generative Models?
entry.content:
Generative AI has changed how we interact with systems. Writing emails, summarising documents, chatting with copilots - all of that is now table stakes. 

But when I work with enterprises, I keep seeing the same pattern: The most important business decisions are not made in text. They are made in tables. 

Employees, sales deals, payments, supply chains, logistics, finance - these are structured datasets. Rows. Columns. Relationships. And this is where many AI initiatives quietly struggle. 

Gartner puts some hard numbers behind this reality: 

  1. 57% of organisations admit their data is not AI‑ready 
  2. Less than 30% of CEOs are satisfied with the returns from current AI investments 
  3. By 2027, task‑specific AI models are expected to outperform general‑purpose LLMs by 3x for decision accuracy 

The problem isn’t AI ambition. It’s using the right kind of AI for the right kind of data. 

Why Generative AI Alone Falls Short for Enterprise Decisions?

Large language models are excellent at natural language tasks. They predict the next word incredibly well. But enterprise systems don’t need the next word. They need the next outcome. 

When applied to structured data, general LLMs often struggle with: 

  1. Numeric accuracy 
  2. Understanding relationships across multiple fields 
  3. Explainability and governance 

That’s not a failure of the technology. It’s simply a mismatch. Structured data requires relational intelligence, not linguistic fluency. 

The Case for Relational Foundation Models 

This is where SAP RPT‑1 becomes especially relevant. 

Play around with SAP RPT-1 at https://rpt.cloud.sap/

RPT1- PlaygroundSample Use CasesTest and Try

SAP RPT‑1 is not an LLM. It is a relational pre‑trained transformer, built specifically for tabular enterprise data. Instead of predicting text, it predicts business outcomes by understanding how columns and rows influence one another. 

In practice, that difference is profound.  Let me illustrate using a few real‑world use cases I’ve been working with. 

Use Case 1: Employee Attrition Risk Intelligence 

One of the most powerful scenarios is employee attrition prediction. I have built this app EARI - using SAP RPT-1 and vibe coding again using SAP BTP.

Attaching the sample screenshots for reference. I will be sharing a blog on how to build it soon. 

EARI - Dashboard

SAP RPT-1 Prediction Attrition Risk and Passing Recommendations

LLM Based Chatbot Integrated and Built using SAP BTP AI Core

Here, employees are not reduced to sentiment or survey comments alone. Instead, the model looks at structured signals such as: 

  • Tenure 
  • Salary vs market benchmark 
  • Overtime hours 
  • Months since last promotion 
  • Engagement score 
  • Performance rating 
  • Training hours 
  • Manager changes 

Based on these inputs, the model produces: 

  • A quantified attrition risk score 
  • Clear risk categories (low, medium, high) 
  • Key risk drivers contributing to the score 
  • Actionable retention recommendations 

For example: 

  • Below‑market salary and excessive overtime surfaced as dominant risk drivers 
  • The system recommended compensation review, workload redistribution, and targeted learning interventions 

This moves HR conversations from gut feel to data‑backed decisions, without treating people like black‑box probabilities. 

Use Case 2: Deal Win Probability in Sales 

Another scenario focuses on sales decision‑making. 

Deal Win Predicator App

Sales teams typically juggle: 

  1. Deal value 
  2. Time in pipeline 
  3. Competitor presence 
  4. Product fit 
  5. Decision‑maker engagement 
  6. Budget confirmation 
  7. Historical win/loss patterns 

Instead of reacting late in the cycle, a predictive model can estimate win probability early and explain why. 

In this scenario: 

  • Structured deal data was analysed 
  • A probability score was generated
  • Clear recommendations followed, such as focusing on ROI articulation or escalating executive engagement 

The result is not automation replacing sellers, but AI augmenting judgement — exactly where enterprise AI should land. 

Use Case 3: From Dashboards to Decisions 

Across HR, sales, finance, and supply chain, there’s a common shift happening. 

Static dashboards answer: “What happened?” 

Predictive, relational models answer: “What is likely to happen next — and what should we do about it?” 

Gartner refers to this evolution as Decision Intelligence, and predicts that by 2027, AI‑driven decision systems will augment or automate 50% of enterprise decisions. 

This is not about replacing human decision‑making. It’s about improving decision quality at scale.

Trust, Governance, and Enterprise Reality 

Another important aspect often overlooked in AI discussions is trust. 

  • Enterprise AI must be: 
  • Explainable 
  • Governed 
  • Secure 
  • Compliant 

Relational models implemented within enterprise platforms make this feasible. Data stays within controlled boundaries, predictions are traceable, and outcomes can be validated — something that becomes critical when AI directly influences people, revenue, or compliance‑sensitive processes. 

Gartner consistently stresses that AI without trust is a liability, not an advantage. 

So Where Does This Leave Generative AI? 

Generative AI is not going away - and it shouldn’t. 

It excels at: 

  1. Conversational interfaces 
  2. Summarisation 
  3. Documentation 
  4. Knowledge assistance 

But for enterprise decisions, generative AI alone is not enough. 

The future is hybrid: 

  1. Generative models for interaction and experience 
  2. Relational foundation models for prediction and decisions 

When these are combined thoughtfully, AI stops being a demo and starts becoming a business capability. 

Final Thought 

The biggest shift I’m seeing isn’t about technology.  It’s about mindset. 

  • From experimentation to outcomes 
  • From dashboards to decisions 
  • From generic AI to domain‑specific intelligence 

Enterprises that recognise this early will move faster, decide better, and waste less time chasing AI hype that never translates into value. 

And in my experience, that’s where the real transformation begins. Even for me as a developer for almost a decade and then another decade being a customer champion, it took a lot of time to accept, adapt and leverage such potential of AI. Now for me the Business Technology Platform is a sleeping bed - From starting my day to closing I observe and leverage different AI advancements in SAP BTP and help our customers win with SAP.

Happy to have a discussion on how does AI help you and your business.

entry.title: Problem witch exchange rate for Electronic Bank statement in Fiori App
entry.content:
I user user exit ZXF01U01 for change exchange rate when importin EBS by FF_5.

Here everything works fine whie postring document in Area 1 and Area 2.

But when I using Fiori App Bank Statement then only in Area 1 exchange rate is changed. In Area 2 document has wrong exchange rate (read from TCURR) but not from my user exit. How to correct it? Substitution And BTE1120 for this field don't work correctly.

e_febep-kursf = lv_rate.
e_febep-fnam1 = 'BKPF-KURSF'.
e_febep-fkoa1 = '0'.
e_febep-fval1 = e_febep-kursf.
CONDENSE e_febep-fval1 NO-GAPS.
REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF '.' IN e_febep-fval1 WITH ','.
e_febep-fnam2 = 'BKPF-KURSF'.
e_febep-fkoa2 = '1'.
e_febep-fval2 = e_febep-fval1.
ENDIF.

entry.title: Erfolgreicher zweiter Stammtisch Basel - Freiburg / Edition Freiburg
entry.content:
Der zweite Stammtisch hat gestern stattgefunden - das erste Mal in Freiburg. Und, trotz starker Konkurrenz durch das Pokalhalbfinalspiel, war er gut besucht. 

In gemütlicher Atmosphäre haben wir uns kennengelernt und uns über SAP und Entwicklung und KI und vieles mehr ausgetauscht. Es war sehr schöner Abend, den wir gerne wiederholen werden - da waren sich alle einig. 

Der einzige Wermutstropfen war am Ende, später am Abend, die Niederlage des SC Freiburgs. Nachdem man das 0:1 noch bejubelt hatte...

Und aus unserer Sicht funktioniert das mit dem SAP Stammtisch auch multinational hervorragend. 

Bastian

entry.title: How to Prepare for Your System-Based SAP BW/4HANA Certification
entry.content:
SAP BW, especially in its SAP-HANA-optimized version, SAP BW∕4HANA, is still the most widely used data warehousing solution with SAP. Consultants for SAP BW/4HANA are continuously highly requested by many enterprises. Whether you want to help within such SAP BW/4HANA projects, want to demonstrate your knowledge within your own organization, or apply for a new position – by proving your skills in this area, you can increase your chances.

 

So how do you prove your expertise?  Understanding the certification format

You may have read or heard that SAP has re-designed the “SAP Certified - Data Engineer - SAP BW/4HANA” and the “SAP Certified - SAP BW/4HANA Delta“ certification. You now need to perform system-based tasks in a real system to verify that you possess the core skills required in the role of a Data Engineer in the SAP BW/4HANA area. It’s not just about memorizing concepts but actually applying them in a system. Check out this page with FAQ and an onboarding video that explains it:

https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-associate-reporting-modelling-and-data-acquisition-with-sap-bw-4hana/exam?onboarding=true

You won’t be asked to simply define terms, but you’ll need to do the work, within a time limit of 180 minutes. This means, your preparation must be heavily practice oriented. Unlike theoretical exams, our hands-on certifications test your ability to navigate the SAP environment, build data models, and demonstrate real-world competency.

You should especially be able to demonstrate the following skills:

  • Creating sources and provisioning data from them,
  • Creating physical and virtual SAP BW/4HANA models,
  • Creating a data flow and filling data targets,
  • Applying concepts like data transformation, including data lookup, unit conversion, and currency translation,
  • Creating queries in SAP BW/4HANA,
  • Implementing authorizations in SAP BW/4HANA.

On the other hand, like in a real project situation, it’s now an open book exam: You are allowed to use any material or online resource to guide you, for example to find a transaction code or table name.

But don’t rely on looking up concepts or practical procedures: There is not much time to complete the certification tasks.

 

How Should You Prepare?

  1. Review the theory:

Before diving into system-based preparation, it’s essential to understand what you’re preparing for.

Even though the exam is practical, you still need to understand the underlying concepts. Make sure you’re comfortable with:

  • Standard SAP BW architecture and exceptional options,
  • Data modeling layers (Staging, Harmonization, Reporting),
  • The purpose of specific objects and object types (e.g., InfoObjects vs ADSOs vs CompositeProviders),
  • Data flow processes (Extraction → Transformation → Loading)

 

  1. Perform our exercises:

Even though the exam tasks are different from the course exercises (which means, copying existing solution objects will not work for you), they are inspired by them. Therefore, invest most of your preparation time into repeating the exercises of our instructor-led trainings BW405, BW410, BW430, BW450, and BW465 – or demonstrations within the corresponding online material. Check the following blog for a list of the required courses:

https://community.sap.com/t5/data-and-analytics-learning-group-blog-posts/how-to-learn-sap-bw-4hana-and-get-certified/ba-p/14307472

 

Make sure that you know where you find the settings needed to fulfil the requirements of these exercises.

 

If you want to practice outside the setting of a classroom training, book an SAP Practice System similar to the ones that are used in the instructor-led class. Click here:

https://learning.sap.com/practice-systems?page=1

 

  1. Practice your own example scenarios:

Some task steps of the system-based certification depend on earlier steps of the same certification. Therefore, try to think about a larger scenario. For example:

  • Create a DataSource in a source system
  • Replicate it to SAP BW.
  • Load data from the source.
  • Transform it.
  • Store it in an ADSO.

 

  1. Timing and Quality management:

Hands-on exams are time-bound. It’s not enough to know how to do something—you must do it efficiently.

  • Practice tasks with a timer.
  • Identify steps where you slow down and intensify your training here.
  • Learn shortcuts and navigation tricks. For example, try to start a new exercise while a loading mechanism takes time without forgetting to return to the result for final steps.
  • Simulate exam conditions as much as possible.
  • Let someone else give you tasks and try to fulfil them as good as you can, even if something might be unclear to you.

In the system-based assessment, you will not be notified if you made an error. At the end, you submit the entire work, and then, there is no chance to correct it. So, focus on ways to test yourself if the objects you created really work as desired.

The technical object names are a crucial part of our verification mechanism. Train to be careful when creating the object names with the correct name. Pay attention to “_” (underscore).

Create a checklist of your common errors and review it before the exam.

 

  1. Live Sessions

In addition to courses and practice systems, the SAP Learning portal offers live sessions that are useful for learning guidelines. Use especially the “Get Certified with BW/4HANA” series, starting on May 5th to ask questions. Click here to book it:

https://learning.sap.com/live-sessions/get-certified-sap-certified-data-engineer-sap-bw-4hana  

These sessions are delivered by subject-matter-experts (SMEs) from SAP Training

Just click below to explore our live sessions:

https://learning.sap.com/live-sessions 

 

 

Taking the Certification Exam

When you feel prepared enough, it's time to take the certification exam.

Just click below for information and booking the certifications:

https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-specialist-sap-bw-4hana-delta

https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-associate-reporting-modelling-and-data-acquisition-with-sap-bw-4hana

Book a time (see the image) when you will not be disturbed. 

 

Make sure that you take the exam in a place where you won’t be interrupted and with good internet. (If the internet connection is lost, you can log in again without losing your objects, but you’ll lose time.)

If something goes wrong:

  • Don’t panic
  • Check previous steps systematically
  • Fix the issue and move on

Remember: the exam is testing your practical problem-solving ability—not perfection 70% is enough.

Make sure that you really submit your work before the time is up.

 

Community

We are looking forward to getting your feedback on our certification and on our resources. Please feel free to use this community and connect with us and other learners and to ask your questions. Also let us know about topics you would like to have covered in future live sessions, so that our content fits your needs even better.

Good luck with your certification journey!

Alexander Scivos

entry.title: Advanced Returns - Query in 1Z3 (Direct Shipment to Supplier) process
entry.content:

Thank you for looking into this case.

We have a query in process 1Z3 - Direct Shipment to Supplier.

As per the process, below are the steps:

  1. Create returns order
  2. Create returns purchase order (automatically)
  3. Perform logistical return of products from customer directly to supplier
  4. Perform logistical goods receipt at supplier side
  5. Receive credit memo (seller) from supplier
  6. Issue credit memo or replacement (seller) to customer

Thank you,

Srujana.

entry.title: Re: How to create Current Transformer wih 3 Ratio.
entry.content: Dear Ajay, usually, Current transformers will have primary to secondary ratio like 1000/400, 1000/5, and 1000/2 in amperes. Depending upon the equipment which is handling this data the tapping is used. For instance, if a KWHr meter has input mentioned as 0-5A, 5A tapping will be used with Multiplication factor of 200. Can you please explain your scenario? I am not clear about your billing factor..

entry.title: .NET 10 applications no longer working with SQL Anywhere
entry.content:
All of our applications using SQL Anywhere with .NET no longer work after upgrading to .NET 10. They're getting stuck as soon as we want to connect to the database.

This is related to https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/support-for-net-core-data-provider-v9/qaq-p/14319973

Switching to x86 lets us connect to the database again, but going 32-Bit for large-scale web applications is not an option.

With .NET 8 support ending soon, we require a fix for this.

entry.title: Automating SAP Kernel Upgrades – A Beginner's Guide to Doing It the Smart Way
entry.content:
What is SAP Kernel?

Think of the SAP Kernel as the engine of your SAP system. Just like a car engine powers everything under the hood, the SAP Kernel is the core executable layer that runs all SAP processes.

Considerations -

It consists of a set of binary executable files (programs) located in the directory:

/usr/sap/<SID>/SYS/exe/uc/linuxx86_64/

Some key executables you will find here include:- 

--disp+work — The SAP Dispatcher, responsible for managing work processes
--msg_server — The SAP Message Server
--enq_server — The Enqueue Server (manages locks)
--sapcontrol — Used to control and monitor SAP instances
--R3trans — Used for transport and DB connectivity checks

The SAP Kernel is independent of the SAP application (ABAP stack). This means you can upgrade the kernel without touching your business data or configurations.

Why Do We Upgrade the SAP Kernel?

SAP regularly releases new kernel patches and versions. Here are the main reasons why you should keep your kernel up to date:

1. Security Patches
SAP releases kernel updates to fix security vulnerabilities. Keeping your kernel patched is critical to protect your system from threats.

2. Bug Fixes
Known issues and bugs in the kernel are resolved in newer versions. If you are experiencing strange system behavior, a kernel upgrade is often the first recommended step.

3. Performance Improvements
Newer kernel versions often include performance optimizations that can improve system throughput and response times.

4. Compatibility
When upgrading to newer SAP releases or applying Support Packages, a minimum kernel version is often required.

5. SAP Support
SAP will only provide support for systems running kernel versions that are within their maintenance window.

Manual vs Automated Approach -

Manual Approach--

The traditional way to perform a kernel upgrade involves the following steps:

1. Log in to the SAP server as root or <sid>adm
2. Stop the SAP system manually using stopsap
3. Create a backup of the existing kernel folder
4. Download the new kernel SAR files from SAP Launchpad
5. Extract the SAR files using SAPCAR
6. Copy the extracted files to the kernel directory
7. Run saproot.sh to set the correct permissions
8. Start the SAP system using startsap
9. Verify the new kernel version using disp+work

While this works perfectly fine, it has a few drawbacks:

- It is time-consuming, especially if you manage multiple systems
- It is error-prone — a missed step or a typo can cause issues
- There is no automatic logging, so troubleshooting is harder
- You have to manually monitor whether SAP has stopped or started

Automated Approach

By automating the kernel upgrade using a shell script, you get:

- Consistency — the same steps are executed every time, without human error
- Logging — every action is recorded in a timestamped log file
- Validation — the script checks whether SAP has stopped before proceeding
- Error handling — if any step fails, the script aborts immediately and tells you what went wrong
- Time savings — especially valuable when managing multiple SAP systems

The Automation Script — Walkthrough

Let us now walk through the improved kernel upgrade script step by step.

Configuration Block

SID="<SID>"
INST_NR="00"
KERNEL_DIR="/usr/sap/${SID}/SYS/exe/uc/linuxx86_64"
NEW_KERNEL_DIR="/usr/sap/${SID}/SYS/exe/uc/new_kernel"
EXTRACTED_DIR="${NEW_KERNEL_DIR}/extracted"
LOG_DIR="/tmp"

Before running the script, you only need to set the SID and instance number at the top. All paths are derived automatically from these values. This makes the script reusable across different SAP systems.

Logging

log() {
echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')] $1" | tee -a "${LOG_FILE}"
}

Every action is written to both the terminal and a log file. The log file is named with a timestamp so you always have a record of what happened and when.

Step 1 — Stop SAP
su -c "/usr/sap/${SID}/SYS/exe/uc/linuxx86_64/sapcontrol -nr ${INST_NR} -function StopSystem" ${sid}adm

The script stops the SAP system as the <sid>adm user. A 30-second sleep is added to give the system time to begin the shutdown process.

Step 2 — Validate SAP is Stopped

while true; do
RUNNING=$(su -s /bin/sh -l ${sid}adm -c \
"sapcontrol -nr ${INST_NR} -function GetProcessList 2>/dev/null" \
| grep -E "GREEN|YELLOW")
if [ -z "$RUNNING" ]; then
log "SAP system is fully stopped."
break
fi
...
done

Rather than blindly waiting, the script actively polls sapcontrol every 10 seconds. If it sees any GREEN or YELLOW processes, it knows SAP is still running. Once all processes are gone, it proceeds. A 5-minute timeout protects against infinite loops.

Step 3 — Backup the Existing Kernel

cp -pfr ${KERNEL_DIR} ${BACKUP_DIR}

A full copy of the existing kernel directory is taken before any changes are made. The backup folder is named with a timestamp so you can always roll back if something goes wrong.

Step 4 — Extract SAR Files

for SAR_FILE in "${NEW_KERNEL_DIR}"/*.SAR; do
${SAPCAR} -xvf "${SAR_FILE}" -R ${EXTRACTED_DIR}
done

The script loops through all SAR files in the new_kernel directory and extracts them one by one using SAPCAR. Each extraction is validated — if one fails, the script aborts immediately.

Note: Make sure SAPCAR is renamed to lowercase "sapcar" before running the script.

Step 5 — Copy to Kernel Directory

cp -pfr ${EXTRACTED_DIR}/. ${KERNEL_DIR}/

The extracted files are copied into the active kernel directory, replacing the old binaries.

Step 6 — Verify Kernel Version

${KERNEL_DIR}/disp+work

The new kernel version is logged so you can confirm the correct version is now in place.

Step 7 — Run saproot.sh

sh "${KERNEL_DIR}/saproot.sh" "${SID}"

This script sets the correct ownership and permissions on the kernel executables. It must be run as root. Skipping this step will cause SAP to fail to start.

Step 8 — Wait for Database

R3trans -d

Before starting SAP, the script checks that the database is available by running R3trans. A return code of 0000 means the database is online and ready. The script waits up to 10 minutes.

Step 9 — Start SAP

su -c "/usr/sap/${SID}/SYS/exe/uc/linuxx86_64/sapcontrol -nr ${INST_NR} -function StartSystem " ${sid}adm

Finally, SAP is started. The script logs the completion with a summary showing the SID, backup location, and log file path.

How to Use the Script

1. Download the new kernel SAR files from SAP Software Downloads (formerly SAP Launchpad) and place them in:

/usr/sap/<SID>/SYS/exe/uc/new_kernel/

2. Rename SAPCAR to lowercase sapcar in the same folder.

3. Edit the script and set your SID and instance number at the top.

4. Run the script as root:

sh Kernel_Upgrade.sh

5. Monitor the output on screen or check the log file in /tmp/.

Things to Keep in Mind

- Always take a backup before starting — the script does this automatically, but verify the backup exists before proceeding.
- Test in a non-production system first — never run automation scripts directly on production without testing.
- Downtime window — coordinate with your team and stakeholders before stopping a production SAP system.
- saproot.sh must be run as root — not as <sid>adm.

 

Conclusion -

Automating the SAP Kernel Upgrade is a great way to save time, reduce errors, and bring consistency to a routine but critical task. As a Basis beginner, understanding each step of the process is just as important as automating it — so I encourage you to read through the script carefully and understand what each section does before running it.

I hope this blog was helpful. If you have any questions or suggestions, feel free to leave a comment below. Happy upgrading!

 

Author: Subham Banerjee
Tags: #SAP Basis#Kernel Upgrade#Shell Script#Automation#SAP Administration

entry.title: Different populations using SF Recruiting and SmartRecruiters within same organization

entry.title: Re: How to generate the MT101 XML file for MBC in SAP HAN...
entry.content:
Hi Joan,

From an SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity perspective, MBC does not generate or convert payment formats. MBC acts as a secure transport layer and processes whatever file format is generated by the sending system, as long as it matches the agreed interface with the bank.

The generation of MT101 (TXT or XML) and any conversion from MT (TXT) to XML is handled entirely within SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition or via additional middleware or conversion logic outside MBC. SAP MBC does not provide an MT101 XML schema or a built‑in conversion tool.

For supported payment formats and responsibilities, you may refer to: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY

For details on payment format generation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (including scope item 16R), I would recommend reviewing the S/4HANA Cloud payment documentation or checking with your SAP FI/payments team.

Best regards
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: SAP MBC for Cloud foundary
entry.content:
Just to add some clarification from an SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity perspective:

 

SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity runs as an SAP‑managed service on BTP, and customers do not deploy or operate it directly in Cloud Foundry. Once MBC is provisioned, it provides its own integration endpoints which can be consumed by SAP S/4HANA Cloud or other SAP systems.

 

SAP Cloud Integration (Integration Suite) is not required for standard MBC usage, unless the customer designs additional, customer‑specific integration flows outside of MBC’s standard scope.

 

For an overview of the supported architecture, please refer to:
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY

Regards,
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: Payment Run/Valdiation of IBAN and Blacklists
entry.content:
MBC does not perform IBAN validation or sanctions/blacklist checks. IBAN validation is handled within S/4HANA Finance during BP maintenance/payment runs, while sanctions screening is typically covered by SAP GTS or FSCM. SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity acts as a transmission layer after the payment file is generated.

Regards,
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: Missing Revenue Recognition Key - Professional Services
entry.content:
Dear Tom,

Event-Based Revenue Recognition does not provide an option to manually assign a revenue recognition key to an object for which the key was not determined by the system. In such cases, the only available approach is to reinitiate the determination process from the external component, provided the object still allows for this.

Please consider the following example:

First, ensure that the required rules for revenue recognition key derivation are properly maintained in SSCUI 102517 – Derivation of Recognition Key for Projects.

For a Professional Services scenario, proceed as follows:

  1. Open the Plan Customer Projects (F0719) app and search for the relevant project.
  2. Switch to Edit mode and navigate to the Billing tab.
  3. If still possible (dependent on the current billing status), change the combination of Contract Type and Product (for example, from Time and Expenses to Fixed Price) and save your changes. Saving the change triggers a new revenue recognition key determination.
  4. Change the combination back to the original Contract Type and Product and save again.
  5. As a result, the revenue recognition key is determined again for the original combination.

Alternatively, you may only perform the first change and then:

  • Open the Event-Based Revenue Recognition Monitoring (F4767) app,
  • Search for the relevant project or WBS billing element, and
  • Use the Change Revenue Recognition Key action.

Kind regards,

Petr

entry.title: Re: Use of Service Materials (DIEN) in Advanced Intercomp...
entry.content: Scope Item 5D2 (Advanced Intercompany Sales) is designed primarily for goods (stock materials like FERT) and relies on valuated stock-in-transit logic, which does not apply to DIEN (service) material types. Using DIEN directly is not supported. The recommended workaround is to model services as non-stock, non-valuated materials (e.g., a custom material type copied from FERT) so that the intercompany billing and postings trigger correctly. For pure service flows, SAP suggests using alternative scope items such as intercompany service scenarios rather than 5D2

entry.title: Re: Multi-Bank Connectivity: usual "banking partner"
entry.content:
Hi Daniel,

 

That’s a very valid question and a common point of confusion when first looking at SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity.

 

In both scenarios (usual banking partner or SWIFT), there is indeed a third party involved. The simplification comes from where and how that dependency is managed.

 

When using the “usual banking partner” option with SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity, SAP operates a single, managed connectivity layer on behalf of the customer. Instead of each customer building and maintaining individual host‑to‑host connections with each bank (different protocols, certificates, security setups), the customer connects once to SAP MBC. SAP then manages the bank‑specific connectivity, security, and protocol differences behind the scenes.

 

With SWIFT, the connection is also centralized, but the customer typically needs a SWIFT membership or a service bureau relationship, and SWIFT‑specific governance and setup applies. That model is well suited if SWIFT is already part of the customer’s landscape, but it introduces its own operational and cost considerations.

 

So the key benefit of the “usual banking partner” option is not removing third parties altogether, but reducing complexity for the customer by:
- Centralizing bank connectivity through SAP
- Avoiding multiple host‑to‑host integrations
- Offloading ongoing maintenance (certificates, security, protocol updates) to SAP

 

From the customer’s perspective, SAP MBC acts as the single integration point regardless of whether banks are connected directly or via networks like SWIFT.

 

For an overview of the supported connectivity models, you may find this helpful:
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY

 

Best regards,
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: SAP Ariba Invoicing 2605 Release Key Innovations
entry.content:
Hello SAP Ariba Community!  We’re excited to share the latest enhancements in SAP Ariba Invoicing (formally SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (CIM)) coming in the Q2 2026 release! As part of our ongoing commitment to innovate and streamline invoice management, we’re happy to announce these key enhancements which are designed to simplify the user experience and operations as well as deliver greater efficiencies overall to our customers.  We hope you enjoy these highlights!

1.  Support of Ad Hoc Approvers in the Approval Workflow (CIM-2578)

In Accounts Payable, exceptions are normal. An unexpected requirement such as an urgent quarter-end invoice can require extra eyes. Until now, that often meant stopping and reconfiguring an approval workflow or sending an invoice back to the start.

In this release, we’re introducing an important capability that many of our users have been asking for: the ability to add or remove approvers in SAP Ariba Invoicing to help your teams handle exceptions without slowing down the process. It’s a simple feature that has a big impact on continuity, cycle times, and on the overall user experience.

Support of Ad Hoc Approvers in the Approval Workflow:

  • You can add and remove ad hoc approval steps while a supplier invoice is already in the approval process.
  • Previously, approvers could only be configured before the workflow started in Workflows for Supplier Invoice Approval.
  • You can dynamically add or remove up to 10 additional approval steps during the approval process to address real-time needs and keep work moving.

Invoice approvals rarely follow a perfect script. This enhancement gives AP, procurement, and finance the flexibility to adapt to unexpected situations while maintaining control.

Key business benefits include:

  • Faster cycle times: Handle exceptions in-flight instead of restarting or reconfiguring the workflow. This keeps critical invoices moving and reduces end-of-period bottlenecks.
  • Better control and compliance: Insert the right approvers at the right time for sensitive categories, high-value invoices, or regulatory checks—without bypassing governance.
  • Less rework: Avoid sending invoices back to drafting or re-submitting just to change approvers. Reduce back-and-forth between AP and requesters.
  • Improved collaboration: Bring in subject matter experts or temporary approvers when needed, then remove those steps once the exception is resolved.    

Below is an example to visualize this enhancement:         

2.  Support of Approvers Based on Spend Limits (CIM-3050)

With this feature, you can now add additional approvers to a supplier invoice based on user approval limits. It allows you to automatically escalate supplier invoices to approvers in the approval hierarchy when the invoice gross amount exceeds an approver's defined limit.

How it works at a glance:

  • You define approval limits for users or roles and maintain your approval hierarchy in SAP Ariba Invoicing.
  • When the invoice gross amount exceeds the current approver’s limit, the system automatically adds the next approver(s) in the hierarchy until the right authorization level is reached.
  • The feature augments your existing approval rules to improve coverage without adding complexity for end users.

This feature introduces important benefits and controls:

  • Stronger financial control: Enforce approval policies consistently by making sure invoices are always reviewed by approvers with the appropriate spending authority.
  • Faster cycle times: Reduce back-and-forth and rerouting caused by invoices landing with approvers who don’t have sufficient limits.
  • Improved compliance and auditability: Create a clear, auditable trail that shows why and how an invoice moved up the chain.
  • Scalability as you grow: As organizations change, limits and hierarchies can be updated without reworking every approval flow.

In the screenshot, you can see the new Approvers based on the spend limit field:

3.  Enhancement to Invoice Rejection Reason (CIM- 3439)

With this release, we’ve introduced clearer supplier notifications for invoice rejections in SAP Ariba Invoicing.  Exception handling is part of every accounts payable operation. When you need to reject an invoice, it should be clear who gets notified and easy to prove that the notification went out. With this release, we have made this simpler and more transparent.

What’s new:

  • Enhancement to Invoice Rejection Reason: When you click Reject Invoice, the system now shows the email ID (email address) of the supplier contact who will receive the rejection notification.
  • Automatic confirmation in Comments: After you reject, the invoice’s Comments tab records a system comment that confirms the notification was sent to the supplier, including the supplier’s email ID.

Some key benefits include:

  • Greater transparency: AP analysts see exactly who will be notified before they confirm a rejection.
  • Faster dispute resolution: Clear routing reduces back-and-forth and helps suppliers get the right information the first time.
  • Better accountability: A time-stamped comment creates an audit trail without manual notes.
  • Improved supplier experience: Suppliers get a timely, directed notice and can act faster.

The screenshot below displays the Supplier Email that will be notified of the invoice rejection:

These enhancements are intended to demonstrate SAP's commitment to building solutions that support and enable your business process needs.

Stay tuned for more enhancements in SAP Ariba Invoicing to drive efficiency and flexibility in your business process.

entry.title: A Deep Dive into Planned Replenishment in SAP EWM
entry.content:
1. Introduction

Even a minor delay in retrieval can lead to a domino effect of downtime and significant financial hits.

To keep things moving, SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) offers robust replenishment strategies that ensure picking bins never run dry.

I’m writing this blog to show how Planned Replenishment effectively bridges the gap between high-rack reserve storage and primary picking zones. The goal is to create a seamless flow of goods so that frequently requested items are always within reach the moment an order is placed.

2. Understanding Replenishment: The Five Strategic Pillars
Replenishment in SAP EWM is the strategic movement of stock from a bulk or reserve area to a designated fixed bin to facilitate efficient picking. While the objective remains the same, the trigger for these movements varies. The following table summarizes the five primary replenishment methods:

Replenishment Type Description

  • Planned Replenishment Triggered by minimum quantity thresholds at fixed bins; executed at scheduled intervals or via batch jobs.
  • Order-Related Replenishment Driven by specific outbound order requirements; tasks are created immediately after picking tasks.
  • Crate Part Replenishment Focused on Production Supply Area (PSA) integration; typically used in Kanban and PP workflows.
  • Automatic Replenishment System-driven background workflow triggered automatically once stock falls below the minimum threshold.
  • Direct Replenishment Operator-driven process initiated via exception codes during the picking process; no additional approval required.

3. The Logic of Planned Replenishment
Planned Replenishment is a proactive strategy widely adopted across various industry verticals. It functions by monitoring "Minimum Quantity" thresholds maintained at the fixed bin level. When the system detects that stock has dropped below this limit, it identifies a deficit.

From a consultant's perspective, the value of this method lies in its predictability. By utilizing batch jobs, the system can generate replenishment warehouse tasks during low-activity periods, ensuring the picking area is fully staged for the upcoming shift without manual intervention or operational bottlenecks.

4. Step-by-Step Configuration
To implement a reliable Planned Replenishment process in Warehouse TECO, the following back-end configurations must be maintained.
1. Activate Replenishment Strategy in Storage Type
Path: EWM → Internal Warehouse Movements → Replenishment Control → Activate Replenishment Strategy in Storage Type
The system must know which storage types are eligible for restocking. For Warehouse TECO, we activate multiple replenishment strategies across different storage types. The overview below shows all configured entries:

For Storage Type 0050 (Fixed Bin Storage), we activate the following four strategies, each serving a different replenishment scenario. For each strategy, Warehouse Process Type 3010 is assigned and "Qty Type Used" is set to Physical Quantity, and "WT Immed." is checked:

Planned Replenishment - Storage Type 0050 (Fixed Bin Storage):

Automatic Replenishment - Storage Type 0050 (Fixed Bin Storage):

Order-Related Replenishment - Storage Type 0050 (Fixed Bin Storage):

Crate Part Replenishment - Storage Type 1000 (Production Supply):

2. Maintain Document and Item Types for Replenishment Warehouse Request
Path: EWM → Internal Warehouse Movements → Replenishment Control → Maintain Document Type / Item Type for Replenishment Warehouse Request
This configuration defines the Warehouse Request that the system generates to move the stock. For Warehouse TECO, Document Type SRPL and Item Type SRPL are assigned for WPT 3010 (Replenishment), and Document Type SPS / Item Type SDPS are assigned for WPT 3100 (Crate Part Replenishment). This ensures the system treats the movement as an internal replenishment rather than a standard outbound delivery.

3. Define Warehouse Process Type (WPT 3010 – Replenishment)
Path: EWM → Cross-Process Settings → Warehouse Task → Define Warehouse Process Type
Define WPT 3010 "Replenishment." Within the WPT settings, the Warehouse Process Category is set to "3" (Internal Warehouse Movement) and the Activity is "REPL." Crucially, the Doc. Type Whse-Intrnl and Item Type Whse-Intrnl must be linked to SRPL to bridge the warehouse request to the physical movement.

4. Define Control Indicators for Determining WPT
Path: EWM → Cross-Process Settings → Warehouse Task → Define Control Indicators for Determining WPT
Control indicators link the product type determination to the warehouse process type. For Warehouse TECO, indicator FX is configured for "Inbound with Unload and put away SMP ZJJA." This indicator is referenced in the product master and is critical for ensuring the system selects the correct WPT during replenishment movements.

5. Determination of Warehouse Process Type
Path: EWM → Cross-Process Settings → Warehouse Task → Determination of Warehouse Process Type
This configuration maps Document Type and Control Indicator combinations to the correct WPT. For Warehouse TECO, Document Type SRPL with Process Type Determination Indicator FX maps to WPT 3010 with "No Special Process" indicator. This drives the system to correctly determine WPT 3010 whenever a replenishment warehouse request is processed.

6. Define Fixed Storage Bin with Min/Max Quantities
T Code: /SCWM/BINMAT
A Fixed Bin must be assigned for the product in the relevant storage type, along with Maximum Quantity and Minimum Quantity thresholds. For Product CABLES in Storage Type 0050, we define Maximum Quantity = 100 EA and Minimum Quantity = 10 EA. The Planned Replenishment run will trigger whenever the physical quantity drops below 10 EA, and will replenish up to the maximum of 100 EA.

7. Maintain Warehouse Product Master Data (PTDI, PACI, SRCI)
T.Code: /SCWM/MAT1
The product master holds the critical indicators that drive both stock put away and stock removal logic during replenishment:
● Process Type Determination Indicator (PTDI): Set to FX -ensures the correct WPT is selected during movement.
● Put away Control Indicator (PACI): Set to YFIX - this is the link to the Storage Type Search Sequence for Pettaway, directing stock to Storage Type 0050 as the replenishment destination.
● Stock Removal Control Indicator (SRCI): Set to SRCI - defines the picking/removal logic from the reserve area (Storage Type 0010).

The PACI indicator YFIX is the critical link. When replenishment is triggered, the system sees YFIX and references the Storage Type Search Sequence to determine that Storage Type 0050 is the correct destination for the stock. Similarly, SRCI drives the removal of stock from the reserve area (Storage Type 0010).

8. Define Putaway Control Indicator (PACI)
Path: EWM → Strategies → Put away → Define Putaway Control Indicator

The Putaway Control Indicator YFIX is configured for Warehouse TECO and mapped to Storage Type 0050 (Fixed Bin Storage). This indicator instructs the system to direct incoming replenishment stock to the correct storage type during putaway determination.

9. Define Storage Type Search Sequence for Put Away
Path: EWM → Strategies → Put away → Define Storage Type Search Sequence for Put away
The Storage Type Search Sequence for Put Away defines the order in which the system searches for destination storage types when performing a put away. For TECO/0050 (YFIX indicator), the search sequence points to Storage Type 0050 as Sequence No. 1, ensuring replenishment stock is directed to the Fixed Bin storage type.

10. Define Stock Removal Control Indicator (SRCI)

Path: EWM → Strategies → Stock Removal → Define Stock Removal Control Indicator
The Stock Removal Control Indicator SRCI is configured as "STOCK REMOVAL FOR REPL" for Warehouse TECO. This indicator is assigned in the product master and is referenced during the stock removal search to determine which storage type to pull stock from during replenishment.

11. Determine Storage Type Search Sequence for Stock Removal
Path: EWM → Strategies → Stock Removal → Determine Storage Type Search Sequence: Stock Removal
For Warehouse TECO, Stock Removal Control Indicator SRCI is mapped to WPT 3010 with Stock Removal Strategy "REPL." This configuration instructs the system to pull stock from Storage Type 0010 (the high-rack reserve area) when executing a replenishment movement to the Fixed Bin.

The detailed assignment confirms that the REPL storage type search sequence assigns Storage Type 0010 as Sequence No. 1 - the source of stock for the replenishment moves:

5. Execution Walkthrough: Testing the Replenishment Run
To validate the configuration, we perform an end-to-end test for Product CABLES in Warehouse TECO.
Pre-Requisite: Initial Stock Verification
Before executing the replenishment run, we verify the current stock position in the Warehouse Management Monitor. As shown, Storage Type 0010 (reserve/high-rack area) holds 100 EA of CABLES, while Storage Type 0050 (FIXED BIN) holds only 10 EA  exactly at the minimum threshold. Since Planned Replenishment triggers when stock is below the minimum (not equal to it), we must first confirm that stock has dropped below 10 EA.

Execute the Replenishment Run
T.Code: /N/SCWM/REPL

Navigate to T Code /SCWM/REPL. Enter the following selection criteria:
● Warehouse Number: TECO
● Replenishment Strategy: Planned Replenishment
● Party Entitled to Dispose: 1000487
● Product: CABLES

First Run - No Replenishment Required
In the first execution, the system log confirms the replenishment run was started, but returns the message "No replenishment to execute." This is expected behavior: the FIXED BIN currently holds 10 EA, which equals the configured minimum quantity. Since the stock has not yet dropped below the threshold, no deficit is detected, and no warehouse task is created.

Second Run - Deficit Detected, WT Created
After stock in the FIXED BIN is consumed below the 10 EA minimum threshold (e.g., through a picking operation reducing it to 5 EA), the replenishment run is executed again. This time, the system identifies the deficit and proposes the replenishment items:

The system proposes a replenishment quantity of 95 EA for CABLES in Storage Type 0050 (FIXED BIN), using WPT 3010 with immediate WT creation. This quantity bridges the gap from the current 5 EA to the configured maximum of 100 EA (5 + 95 = 100).
The log confirms successful warehouse task creation:

Warehouse Task 603 - Movement Details
Reviewing the generated Warehouse Task 603 in the monitor, we can see the full movement details. The system has created a task to move stock from Source Bin 011.01.01.04 (Storage Type 0010 — high-rack reserve) to Destination Bin FIXED BIN (Storage Type 0050), using Process Type 3010:

The task quantity detail confirms a Source Target Quantity of 95 EA:

Confirm Warehouse Task
To physically execute the stock movement, select WT 603 and choose "Confirm WT in Background" from the context menu. This confirms the warehouse task and completes the stock transfer from the reserve area to the Fixed Bin:

6. Results and Final Verification
After confirming the warehouse task, a final check of the Warehouse Management Monitor confirms that the replenishment has been executed successfully. The "Unrestricted-Use Warehouse" stock in Storage Type 0050 (FIXED BIN) is now exactly 100 EA - fully restocked to the configured maximum. The reserve area (Storage Type 0010) correspondingly reflects 10 EA remaining:

Warehouse Task 603 was created automatically by the Planned Replenishment run. • The system calculated a replenishment quantity of 95 EA, the exact amount needed to reach the Maximum Quantity of 100 EA from the deficit level of 5 EA. • The movement was executed from Source Bin 011.01.01.04 (Storage Type 0010) to Destination Bin FIXED BIN (Storage Type 0050). • Final stock in FIXED BIN: 100 EA fully replenished

7. Conclusion
Through this walkthrough, we validated how SAP EWM Planned Replenishment leverages a chain of tightly integrated configurations - storage type activation, fixed bin min/max settings, process type determination, put away, and stock removal search sequences - to create a fully automated and reliable stock replenishment workflow.

The Planned Replenishment strategy is particularly well-suited for warehouses with predictable demand patterns, such as automotive spare parts environments, where ensuring fixed bin availability is critical to preventing production downtime. When combined with scheduled batch jobs, it provides a hands-free, proactive mechanism that keeps picking locations stocked without manual intervention.

entry.title: Re: Material inconsistencies during WIP step of Material...
entry.content:
Hello Jessica,

The inconsistency error is extremely complex. The error can be caused from the difference between MM-ML table, or MM-FI table, or ML-FI table, or ML-ML table... Only 1 error message (ex: C+048) can be fixed by several solutions/ reports depend on your data. Updating inconsistencies by wrong report can make your data worse. Due to its danger and risky, only SAP Support team can handle to fix and correct the inconsistency.

About pre-check inconsistency before the period end closing, you can use CKMHELP to check, but cannot correct due to above reason.

But as you can see there are a lot of reports in CKMHELP, I cannot suggest which reports should be run for each period end. Because in March you may facing MM-ML inconsistency, but in April you may facing ML-FI inconsistency. So please note that this checking will take you a lot of effort'; and the error from CKMLCP not always come from the inconsistency, but maybe due to missing SAP Note. 

So I think with the inconsistency error, create an OSS, apply Note for permanent solution or Upgrade the system should be recommended.

Have a good day!

entry.title: Re: Automating SAP Kernel Upgrades – A Beginner's Guide to Doing It the Smart Way
entry.content:
thank you for the blog, but can you add the point to copy icmbnd.new to icmbnd, otherwise you will not be able to use ICM ports under 1080. also properties for sapuxuserchk should be manually set

we do the following for that

as root user

cd /sapmnt/<SID>/exe/uc/linuxx86_64
cp -r icmbnd.new icmbnd
chown -R <sidadm>:sapsys /sapmnt/<SID>/exe/uc/linuxx86_64
chmod -R 755 /sapmnt/<SID>/exe/uc/linuxx86_64
chown root:sapsys sapuxuserchk icmbnd
chmod 4750 sapuxuserchk icmbnd

 

best regards

entry.title: Enterprise Transformation Made Simple with SAP Signavio
entry.content:
Hello Readers,

Today we will go through about Enterprise Transformation Made Simple with SAP Signavio.

Today, many organisations are investing in modern ERP platforms like SAP S/4HANA. But just implementing new technology doesn’t automatically bring real change. The true transformation happens when businesses clearly understand their processes, improve them and keep optimising them over time.

SAP Signavio helps organisations to analyse, improve, and continuously manage their business processes for better results.

Enterprise Transformation is required to redesign how a company operates to become process Faster, More Efficient and Data -driven.

Image: Transformation with SAP Signavio.

We will go through example of Manufacturing company-

A typical production company involves processes as below, Typically production company involves business processes such as,

  • Sales & Distribution (OTC) for creation of sales orders.
  • Material Management (P2P) for procurement of materials
  • Production (PP) for Manufacturing, Demand planning, Material requirement
  • Warehouse (WM) for Storage, stock movement, picking & putaway of materials.
  • Finance (FI) for Billing.

Thus, when processes are not clearly defined, it occurs issues in day-to-day activities are as,

  • Delivery of products are not on time.
  • Production activities get delays.
  • Too much manual work reduces efficiency of production.
  • Same errors keep happening again.
  • Poor user experience

1. Start the processes with clear Visibility- In many organisations business processes are not defined properly no documentations are carried out, So teams do not have clear roadmap of the processes how organisation works from start to end.

Because of this occurs issues such as Unable to identify where delay occurs, and Due to no documented, no visibility of complete process.

With Signavio Organisations can map their processes visually and gain a clear view of the entire workflow.

As a result, Bottlenecks become more visible, unnecessary steps can be identified and removed and overall process becomes more easier to manage.

2. Depend on Real data not on Assumptions- Working in organisation In day-to-day work, it’s common to see different teams blaming each other when something gets wrong.

This Problem doesn’t solve because most of these discussions are based on assumptions rather than actual data.

With Signavio we can rely on actual data from ERP system to understand that what is going on in processes. It helps you clearly see where delays are occurring, such as approvals taking two or three days, where manual work is still involved, and where rework or repeated approvals are happening.

As a result, Decisions are based on facts, no guess on assumptions.

3. Make Complex Business Processes Simple- Due to customization, business processes get complicated.

With Signavio identifies and helps to cleaning up the process and bringing it back to a more standard and simple flow.

As result, Errors are reduced and overall production time is reduced.

4. Understand Business Processes Before Moving to SAP S/4HANA- Before starting transformation it is important to understand how current processes are working, Process gaps and Standardised.

With Signavio it helps to compare your existing business processes with SAP best practices and shows clearly shows were unnecessary customisations or complications.

Because of this implementation becomes faster and overall risk is reduced and our process stays standard and simple manageable.

5. User Experience & Continuous Improvements- Improving User Experience and while continuously improving. The transformation is not just about system it is also about the system that people use it daily.

User struggles with manual steps, repeated data entry, complicated approval workflows this increases errors.

With Signavio, Business processes are more user friendly, This reduces the mistakes, improves employee’s satisfaction, Improves user experience.

Conclusion- SAP Signavio help businesses clearly see their complete processes from start to end, understand where things are slowing down using real data, and simplify workflows that have become too complex over time. It also supports a smoother and faster move to business processes. While improving continuous even after Implementation and making transformation practical and long lasting.

entry.title: Romanian Statutory Reports
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

We are currently working on generating the statutory financial reports required in Romania using S/4HANA Public Cloud and the Run Statutory Reports app (SAF‑T Statement 406, Statement 394 – General Ledger Journal, Statement 390, TriaI balance with 4types of equalities, Account sheet for miscellaneous operations ,...).

All configuration has been completed following the official SAP Help documentation (Document and Reporting Compliance). However, the client reports that the standard outputs do not comply with Romanian legal requirements, and several validations have been rejected.

Given that the standard reports are not being accepted, we would like to understand:

  1. Is there any extensibility option (CDS view, BAdI, configuration point, or other mechanism) that allows adapting statutory tax reports for Romania in Public Cloud?
  2. Is there any official SAP note, roadmap item, or localization guideline specifically addressing Romanian statutory reporting compliance?
  3. Has anyone successfully implemented these reports in Public Cloud for Romania and can share insights or known limitations?

We are currently stuck because the client expects legally compliant reports, and we need to confirm whether adaptation is possible or if the standard solution must be used as‑is.

Any guidance, experience, or documentation would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you very much in advance.

Best regards,

Jessica Aguilar

entry.title: Purchase Order Condition Not Included in Total
entry.content:
In the Purchase Order, a custom condition type (tolling cost) has been configured and added to the pricing schema.

The condition is correctly calculated and appears in the Conditions tab (item level).
It is also visible in the PO layout/print preview under the totals section.
However, the issue is:

The tolling cost is not included in the overall total amount calculation of the Purchase Order.
The Total Amount only reflects standard conditions (e.g., base price, freight), while the tolling cost is displayed separately and not aggregated into the final total.

entry.title: Re: Internal Order - Account Assignment Category modifica...
entry.content:
Steps to change Account Assignment Category in Settlement Rule

  1. Go to Transaction KO02.
  2. Enter the internal order number (e.g., 1020304050).
  3. Navigate to Settlement Rule (menu path: Goto → Settlement Rule or use the Settlement Rule button).
  4. In the settlement rule line(s), locate the Account Assignment Category field.
  5. Currently set to MAT (Material).
  6. Change it to ORD (Order).
  7. Adjust the Receiver field accordingly (since ORD requires a valid receiver order number).
  8. Save the changes.

Please note the available Account Assignment Categories depend on customizing in Settlement Profile (transaction OKO7). If ORD is not available, check the settlement profile assigned to your internal order type. Ensure the receiver order exists and is valid for settlement. If the system prevents the change, it may be due to settlement rules already executed or restrictions in customizing.

  2. Select "SAP Best Practices for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition" corresponding to the current version.

entry.title: Asset data migration inconsistent
entry.content:
Dear support,

We are testing asset migration in asset accounting using migration cockpit.

For first test, we migrated one asset from company code 3000. In "Make Company Code Settings - Asset Accounting-Specific" app we have maintained 30.06.2026 as data transfer. Go live will be on july 1st 2026.

The system is calculating depreciation values not consistent with the customizing. In customizing, for the asset class used the useful life is 50 years. For the National Code 2015 used in this asset is 2% tax depreciation. This is correct.

Please see values below:

  • Asset 10000002-0 
  • Capitalized on 01.01.1998
  • Depreciation key PT04
  • Useful life 50 years
  • Depreciation started date 01.01.1998
  • APC Transaction 498 797,90 EUR
  • Ordinary Depreciation by 31.12.2025 -279 326,89 EUR
  • Net book value by 31.12.2025 219 471,01 EUR
  • Expired useful life at 30.06.2026 28 years and 6 months.
  • Posted depreciation by 30.06.2026  -4 987,98 EUR

According to asset master data previously mentioned the yearly depreciation is 498 797,90 EUR/50y = 9975,96 EUR. The system is calculating annual depreciation as 10207,95 EUR instead of 9975,96 EUR. The system is using 2,05% instead of 2%, as configured.

Any ideas?

Thanks, Marlene

entry.title: Re: Purchase Order Condition Not Included in Total
entry.content: The tolling cost condition is excluded from the PO total because it is currently set up as a statistical condition in the pricing schema. To include it in the overall total, update the condition type and pricing procedure so that it contributes to the net value. This ensures the PO total reflects all payable costs.

entry.title: Re: Need Odata API to convert Purchase Req to PO
entry.content:
One workaround which you can try is read the PR details via API_PURCHASEREQ_PROCESS_SRV. Use those details to construct a PO payload and send it via API_PURCHASEORDER_PROCESS_SRV. This effectively replicates the MD04 → ME21N process programmatically.
Alternatively, SAP Fiori apps like Manage Purchase Requisitions allow converting PRs to POs in bulk. While not an API, they provide automation at the UI level.

entry.title: S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Fully Automated Setup for Central Monitoring in SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
What is Central Monitoring in SAP Cloud ALM?

Central Monitoring is a core capability within SAP Cloud ALM for Operations that enables customers to monitor the health, performance, and availability of their SAP systems and services from a single, centralized platform.

SAP Cloud ALM is provided at no additional license cost for customers with SAP Enterprise Support (cloud editions).  Refer to Usage Rights. SAP Cloud ALM can be used across the entire Project lifecycle.”

 For S/HANA Cloud Public Edition customers, Central Monitoring provides:

  • Automated data collection from S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition systems through pre-configured communication scenarios and push-enabled data collection mechanisms
  • Real-time health metrics covering system availability, performance, resource utilization, integration flows, business processes, and security events
  • Integration with multiple monitoring use cases including Health Monitoring, Integration & Exception Monitoring, Business Process Monitoring, Job & Automation Monitoring, and Real User Monitoring
  • Cloud-native architecture built on SAP Business Technology Platform, eliminating the need for on-premise monitoring infrastructure

The integration between S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and SAP Cloud ALM is established through automated setup processes that leverage SAP BTP formations and communication arrangements, significantly reducing configuration complexity compared to traditional monitoring solutions.

An automated standard setup is available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud public edition systems.
This significantly reduces the time and effort required to connect S/HANA Cloud Public Edition systems to SAP Cloud ALM and activate the monitoring use cases for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition in SAP Cloud ALM.

  1. The first step is to check that the prerequisites have been met
  2. The second step is to perform a simple procedure.

Prerequisites

The fully automated setup for SAP S/HANA Cloud Public Edition can be applied if your implementation meets all of the following prerequisites:

  • The system information is automatically imported from the SAP backend (SLIS) to the Landscape Management app of SAP Cloud ALM.
  • In the Landscape Management, the service has the status New.
  • The customer number for SAP S/HANA Cloud Public Edition matches the customer number for the SAP Cloud ALM tenant.
  • SAP Cloud ALM is not located in SAP Data Center eu10-004.

Note: If the system is already active the prerequisites cannot be met, so this cannot be performed retroactively, and then it would already be configured.

Once the prerequisites have been confirmed, then follow the steps in the procedure described next:

Procedure

To activate monitoring in SAP Cloud ALM, follow these 5 steps:

  1. In the Landscape Management app in SAP Cloud ALM, open SAP S/HANA Cloud Public Edition, and choose Central Monitoring.


  2. In the dialog that opens, confirm consent and start the activation process.
  3. These steps occur automatically:
  4. Result: Supported use cases become active in the Landscape Management service in SAP Cloud ALM.

    5. Adjust the monitoring setup within the monitoring apps in SAP Cloud ALM as needed. For more details about configuration, see SAP Cloud ALM for Operations.

    Central Monitoring can also be deactivated if required by sliding the selector button to off

    Deactivation:
    Deactivate the connection in the Landscape Management app by toggling off central monitoring.

Key Benefits

Operational Benefits

  1. Proactive Problem Detection and Resolution
    • Central Monitoring continuously collects and analyzes system metrics to detect anomalies before they impact business operations
    • Automated alerting notifies IT teams immediately when thresholds are breached or exceptional situations occur, enabling faster response times
    • Embedded analytics support root cause analysis by correlating events across multiple system components and providing drill-down capabilities into specific issues

  2. End-to-End Visibility Across Hybrid Landscapes
    • Provides unified monitoring for SAP  S/HANA Cloud Public Edition systems alongside other SAP cloud services and on-premise components in hybrid scenarios
    • Enables correlation of business processes, integrations, and technical metrics across the entire solution landscape
    • Delivers a single pane of glass for monitoring, reducing the need to switch between multiple monitoring tools  

  3. Reduced Operational Complexity and Effort
    • Eliminates the need to deploy, configure, and maintain on-premise monitoring infrastructure
    • Automated setup processes significantly reduce time-to-value compared to traditional monitoring solutions
    • Pre-configured monitoring content and KPIs are available out-of-the-box, minimizing customization requirements

Technical Benefits

  1. Comprehensive Health Monitoring
    • Tracks critical system health indicators including availability, response times, memory and CPU utilization, database performance, disk space usage, and security events
    • Provides historical data retention for trend analysis and capacity planning
    • Supports threshold-based alerting with configurable notification channels

  2. Integration and Exception Monitoring
    • Enables end-to-end monitoring of data exchange between SAP S/HANA Cloud Public Edition and other systems by correlating single messages to complete message flows
    • Provides unified monitoring for both peer-to-peer interfaces and orchestrated integration scenarios
    • Supports efficient problem resolution through integrated search-and-track functionality for individual messages

  3. Business Process Monitoring
    • Monitors the health of end-to-end business processes in real-time using predefined KPIs
    • Enables drill-down into business documents for detailed analysis and reporting
    • Supports creation of custom process structures and KPIs to reflect organizational requirements

Business Benefits

  1. Ensured Business Continuity
    • Minimizes system downtime through proactive detection and rapid resolution of issues
    • Reduces business disruption by identifying and addressing problems before they impact end users
    • Provides transparency into business process execution quality and performance

  2. Improved Decision-Making Through Analytics
    • Delivers actionable insights through embedded analytics and intelligence functionality
    • Supports data-driven decision-making for both IT operations and process governance
    • Enables identification of optimization opportunities and performance bottlenecks

  3. Cost Efficiency and Reduced Total Cost of Ownership
    • Included with SAP Enterprise Support for cloud editions at no additional license cost
    • Eliminates hardware investments and operational costs associated with on-premise monitoring solutions
    • Reduces administrative effort through automation and standardized processes

Monitoring Capabilities Enabled

System Health Monitoring

  • Availability tracking: Continuous monitoring of system uptime and service availability
  • Performance metrics: Real-time collection of response times, throughput, and transaction performance
  • Resource utilization: Monitoring of CPU, memory, disk space, and database performance
  • Security monitoring: Tracking of security events, certificate expiration, and compliance status

Performance Monitoring

  • Real User Monitoring (RUM): Measures actual end-user experience by tracking all user interactions and server requests
  • Synthetic User Monitoring (SUM): Proactively tests application availability and performance through automated script execution
  • Response time analysis: Provides drill-down from global request types to individual execution details  

Integration Monitoring

  • Message flow correlation: Tracks messages from sender to all intended receivers across distributed systems  
  • Exception detection: Identifies and reports integration-related exceptions and errors
  • Search and track functionality: Enables searching for specific messages based on technical and business attributes  

Job and Automation Monitoring

  • Job execution tracking: Monitors background job processing status and performance
  • Queue monitoring: Tracks queue backlogs and processing delays
  • Automation workflow monitoring: Oversees automated operational tasks and workflows

Benefits without/with Central Monitoring

 

Aspect

Without Central Monitoring

With Central Monitoring

Benefit

Monitoring Infrastructure

Requires deployment and maintenance of on-premise monitoring tools

Cloud-native SaaS solution with no infrastructure requirements

Reduced IT overhead and faster time-to-value

Problem Detection

Reactive approach based on user reports or scheduled checks

Proactive, real-time detection with automated alerting

Faster issue identification and resolution

End-to-End Visibility

Fragmented views across multiple tools and systems

Unified monitoring across hybrid landscape from single platform

Improved operational efficiency and decision-making

Setup Complexity

Manual configuration of monitoring scenarios and data collection

Automated setup with pre-configured content and KPIs

Significantly reduced implementation effort

Cost Structure

License fees, hardware costs, and operational expenses

Included with SAP Enterprise Support at no additional cost

Lower total cost of ownership

Analytics Capabilities

Limited or requires separate analytics tools

Embedded analytics with root cause analysis and trend identification

Enhanced troubleshooting and optimization 

Integration Monitoring

Point-to-point monitoring without message correlation

End-to-end message flow tracking across systems

Faster resolution of integration issues 

Business Process Visibility

Technical monitoring only, limited business context

Business process monitoring with KPI tracking and document drill-down

Better alignment between IT and business stakeholders

Conclusion:

Enabling Central Monitoring in SAP Cloud ALM delivers significant operational, technical, and business benefits for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition customers.

 By providing comprehensive, cloud-native monitoring capabilities at no additional license cost, SAP Cloud ALM enables organizations to ensure business continuity, improve operational efficiency, and reduce total cost of ownership

The automated setup process and pre-configured monitoring content minimize implementation effort, while embedded analytics and intelligent alerting accelerate problem detection and resolution

For SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition customers seeking to modernize their monitoring approach and gain end-to-end visibility across hybrid landscapes, Central Monitoring in SAP Cloud ALM represents a strategic investment that delivers measurable value from day one.

Classic Setup

In the event that you do not meet the prerequisites for Central Monitoring, the SAP S/HANA Cloud Public Edition system can still be added using the Classic Setup.
